summary: Jeff Atwood argues that daily builds are no longer ambitious enough given modern hardware capabilities, and developers should instead practice continuous integration with multiple builds per day. He points to the Windows NT project, which managed daily builds despite 5.6 million lines of code and 19-hour build times, as proof that smaller projects have no excuse. The post emphasizes that code should almost always be in a compilable state and that holding files checked out overnight is a red flag. Atwood cites Martin Fowler's arguments that continuous integration catches integration bugs the same day they're introduced, dramatically reducing debugging time.
key_points: The technological argument against frequent builds (time constraints) is obsolete with modern fast processors and distributed build tools
Developers should never hold onto code changes for more than a day, and ideally should integrate every few hours
Code should almost always be in a compilable state - if you can't check in for more than a day, you may have bad programming habits
Continuous integration catches integration bugs the same day they're introduced, making bug hunting far easier
Software should be grown incrementally from a small functional base with aggressive daily check-ins as checkpoints
money_quotes: I believe, for all but the largest projects, you should be building multiple times per day.
I get aggravated when other developers leave files checked out overnight.
If you're writing code that is so broken you can't check any of it in for more than a day – you might have some bad programming habits.
I believe it's far healthier to grow or accrete your software from a small, functional base and use aggressive daily check-ins to checkpoint those growth stages.
Continuous Integration can slash the amount of time spent in integration hell, in fact it can turn hell into a non-event.
Those of us who work on projects of less staggering proportions will have a hard time explaining why we aren't also reaping the benefits of this practice.
key_insight: Modern hardware has eliminated every excuse for not practicing continuous integration, and developers who can't keep their code in a compilable state for more than a day likely have deeper problems with their programming habits.
